skill.name: game-development
skill.description: Game development orchestrator. Routes to platform-specific skills based on project needs.
skill.summary: Comprehensive game development knowledge base covering all platforms, dimensions, and specialty areas.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 11 skill files are documentation-only markdown files containing game development principles and guidelines. Static analysis flagged 67 'weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns and 41 'external_commands' patterns, but these are FALSE POSITIVES caused by markdown formatting (code blocks using backticks) and conceptual examples, not actual executable code. No security risks detected. Safe for publication.

content.value_statement: Game development requires mastering multiple disciplines from physics to art direction. This skill provides structured principles and routing to specialized knowledge for every game development need.
actual_capabilities: Route to platform-specific skills (web, mobile, PC, VR/AR)
Guide game design decisions including core loops and GDD structure
Explain multiplayer networking architecture and synchronization
Cover game art pipelines and animation principles
Address game audio integration and adaptive audio systems
limitations: Does not execute code or run game engines directly
Cannot create actual game assets (sprites, models, audio files)
Recommendations are general principles, not engine-specific tutorials
Does not provide real-time multiplayer server infrastructure

use_cases.title: Indie Developer Starting First Project
use_cases.description: Get guidance on engine selection, core gameplay loop design, and platform-specific optimization for a solo developer building their first commercial game.

use_cases.title: Student Learning Game Development
use_cases.description: Understand fundamental concepts like state machines, collision detection, and camera systems while building portfolio projects.

use_cases.title: Prototyping Game Mechanics
use_cases.description: Quickly prototype game mechanics with AI guidance on patterns like object pooling, input abstraction, and behavior trees before full implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Choose a Game Engine
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to make a 2D platformer game for PC and mobile. Which game engine should I choose and why? Consider my budget and experience level as a beginner.

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Design a Core Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a core gameplay loop for a roguelike deck-builder. What should the 30-second loop look like, and how do I ensure it stays engaging over multiple runs?

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Multiplayer Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm building a competitive 4v4 shooter. Should I use dedicated servers or host-based architecture? Explain the trade-offs for latency, cost, and anti-cheat.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: VR Comfort Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Players report motion sickness in my VR game during teleportation. What comfort settings and design patterns should I implement to reduce nausea while maintaining immersion?

output_examples.input: I want to make a browser-based 2D platformer
output: Start with game-development/web-games for framework selection (Phaser recommended for 2D)
Use game-development/2d-games for sprite animation and tilemap patterns
Reference game-development/game-design for platformer mechanics like coyote time and jump buffering
Key considerations: asset compression for web loading, touch/keyboard input abstraction, PWA for offline play

output_examples.input: How do I optimize my game for mobile battery life?
output: Target 30 FPS instead of 60 FPS for casual games
Implement thermal throttling: reduce quality when device warms
Use object pooling to avoid garbage collection spikes
Sleep completely when app is backgrounded
Minimize GPS and network polling frequency
Use dark mode to save OLED battery

best_practices: Prototype fast with simple shapes, then add art - fun comes first
Use fixed timestep for physics and game logic, interpolate for rendering
Profile before optimizing - measure actual bottlenecks, not assumptions
Abstract input to actions (jump, move) not specific keys or buttons
Design for your target platform constraints from the start
anti_patterns: Optimizing code before confirming it's fun - premature optimization kills iteration
Mixing input handling with game logic - makes multi-platform support difficult
Creating objects in hot loops without pooling - causes garbage collection stuttering
Trusting client input in multiplayer - enables cheating and exploits
Moving the camera without player control in VR - causes motion sickness

faq.question: Which game engine is best for beginners?
faq.answer: Godot 4 is excellent for beginners due to its gentle learning curve, free license, and built-in 2D engine. Unity 6 is also beginner-friendly with extensive tutorials. Unreal 5 has steeper learning but offers AAA visual quality.

faq.question: How do I prevent motion sickness in VR games?
faq.answer: Maintain 90+ FPS consistently, use teleportation instead of smooth locomotion, offer snap turning, add vignette during movement, and never move the camera without player input. Always provide comfort settings.

faq.question: What networking architecture should I use for my multiplayer game?
faq.answer: Competitive games need dedicated servers for fairness and anti-cheat. Casual co-op can use host-based architecture. Turn-based games work well with simple client-server. Never trust the client for authoritative game state.

faq.question: How can I make my game run better on mobile devices?
faq.answer: Target 30 FPS for casual games, use object pooling, simplify shaders, reduce draw calls through batching, compress assets aggressively, and implement thermal throttling. Test on low-end devices early.

faq.question: What is the minimum viable Game Design Document?
faq.answer: Include: one-sentence pitch, core 30-second gameplay loop description, key mechanics list, progression system overview, and visual style reference. Keep it living and update as the game evolves.

faq.question: How do I choose between 2D and 3D for my first game?
faq.answer: 2D is generally easier for beginners - simpler collision, animation, and level design. Start with 2D unless your core concept requires 3D. Many successful indie games use 2D or 2.5D approaches.
